gh-w9-f1.answer:
AuthenticationAPI/ — standalone login service with JWT token generation and validation.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — spring-boot-starter-web, spring-boot-starter-security, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, mysql-connector-j, jjwt-api/jjwt-impl/jjwt-jackson (0.12.x)
• src/main/java/com/academy/auth/
  - AuthApiApplication.java
  - model/User.java — id, username, email, password (hashed), roles (Set<Role>)
  - model/Role.java — enum: USER, ADMIN
  - repository/UserRepository.java — findByUsername, findByEmail
  - security/JwtService.java — generateToken, extractUsername, isTokenValid
  - security/JwtAuthenticationFilter.java — OncePerRequestFilter
  - service/AuthService.java — login logic
  - controller/AuthController.java
  - dto/LoginRequest.java, AuthResponse.java (token + expiry)
• application.properties — server.port=8091, jwt.secret (use env var), jwt.expiration=86400000

Endpoints:
• POST /api/auth/login — body: {"username":"admin","password":"secret123"} → returns JWT + expiresIn
• POST /api/auth/validate — header: Authorization: Bearer <token> → returns user info if valid
• GET /api/auth/me — protected, returns current authenticated user

Key concepts: Authentication = proving identity. JWT is stateless — server signs a token, client sends it on every request. Never return password hash in responses. BCrypt hashes passwords on registration (see RegistrationAPI).
gh-w9-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Every SaaS app has a login endpoint. JWT lets mobile apps and SPAs authenticate without server-side sessions — the pattern used by Netflix, Spotify backends, and most microservices.

Run:
cd AuthenticationAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test login:
POST http://localhost:8091/api/auth/login
Content-Type: application/json
{"username":"admin","password":"admin123"}

Copy token from response, then:
GET http://localhost:8091/api/auth/me
Authorization: Bearer <paste-token-here>

Push to springboot-week-2-security:
git add AuthenticationAPI/
git commit -m "Add AuthenticationAPI — JWT login and validation"
git push

Security tip: Store jwt.secret in environment variable, never commit real secrets.

gh-w9-f2.answer:
RegistrationAPI/ — user registration with BCrypt password hashing, email uniqueness, and role assignment.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — same security + JPA dependencies as AuthenticationAPI
• src/main/java/com/academy/register/
  - RegisterApiApplication.java
  - model/User.java — username, email, password, roles
  - repository/UserRepository.java
  - service/RegistrationService.java — encode password, assign default role
  - controller/RegisterController.java
  - dto/RegisterRequest.java — username, email, password, confirmPassword
  - dto/RegisterResponse.java — id, username, email (no password!)
  - exception/DuplicateEmailException.java
• application.properties — server.port=8092, database auth_db

Endpoints:
• POST /api/register — create new user
• GET /api/register/check-email?email=test@test.com — returns {"available": true/false}
• POST /api/register/admin — bonus: admin-only endpoint to create users with specific roles

Validation rules:
• Password minimum 8 characters, at least one digit
• Email format valid (@Email)
• Username unique, email unique
• password and confirmPassword must match
• Default role: USER

Key concepts: Registration is public (permitAll), login is public, everything else requires JWT. PasswordEncoder bean (BCryptPasswordEncoder) hashes before save — never store plain text.
gh-w9-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: Sign-up flows are the #1 attack surface — weak passwords, duplicate accounts, and email enumeration bugs. BCrypt + validation + unique constraints are baseline security every backend dev must implement.

Run:
cd RegistrationAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test:
POST http://localhost:8092/api/register
{"username":"riya","email":"riya@test.com","password":"Secure123","confirmPassword":"Secure123"}

Verify in MySQL: password column should start with $2a$ (BCrypt hash).

Push:
git add RegistrationAPI/
git commit -m "Add RegistrationAPI — BCrypt registration with validation"
git push

gh-w9-f3.answer:
JWTConfiguration.java — centralized JWT utility class for token generation, parsing, and validation.

Requirements:
1. Read secret key and expiration from application.properties (jwt.secret, jwt.expiration).
2. generateToken(UserDetails userDetails) — create signed JWT with subject=username, issuedAt, expiration, optional roles claim.
3. extractUsername(String token) — parse and return subject.
4. extractExpiration(String token) — return expiry date.
5. isTokenValid(String token, UserDetails userDetails) — check username matches and token not expired.
6. Use io.jsonwebtoken (JJWT 0.12.x) with HMAC-SHA256 signing.
7. Mark as @Component so Spring injects it into filters and services.

Key concepts: JWT has three parts — Header.Payload.Signature. Payload holds claims (sub, iat, exp, roles). Secret key must be at least 256 bits for HS256. Token expiration limits damage if token is stolen.
gh-w9-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: One JwtService used everywhere avoids copy-paste bugs. Production teams rotate secrets via env vars and use short-lived access tokens (15–60 min) plus refresh tokens for longer sessions.

Place in: src/main/java/com/academy/security/JWTConfiguration.java (or JwtService.java — match your project naming).

Configure in application.properties:
jwt.secret=${JWT_SECRET:your-256-bit-secret-key-change-in-production}
jwt.expiration=3600000

Push:
git add JWTConfiguration.java
git commit -m "Add JWTConfiguration — token generation and validation"
git push

gh-w9-f4.answer:
UserService.java — core user management service implementing UserDetailsService and handling user CRUD with security awareness.

Requirements:
1. Implement UserDetailsService — loadUserByUsername(String username) returns Spring Security UserDetails.
2. Map your User entity to UserDetails with encoded password and granted authorities from roles.
3. Methods: findByUsername, findById, findAll (admin only at controller level), updateProfile, changePassword.
4. changePassword — verify old password with passwordEncoder.matches(), encode new password, save.
5. Never expose password field in any DTO or API response.
6. @Transactional on write methods.
7. Optional: deleteUser(Long id) — admin only.

Key concepts: UserDetailsService is the bridge between your database User and Spring Security. loadUserByUsername is called during login authentication. Authorities become roles like ROLE_ADMIN used in @PreAuthorize.
gh-w9-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: UserService is the single source of truth for user data in most apps. Separating it from AuthController keeps login thin and makes unit testing password changes and profile updates straightforward.

Run your auth project and verify loadUserByUsername works when login succeeds.

Push:
git add UserService.java
git commit -m "Add UserService — UserDetailsService and profile management"
git push

Test changePassword via ProfileAPI once wired up.

gh-w9-f5.answer:
SecurityConfiguration.java — Spring Security filter chain configuring authentication, authorization, JWT filter, and password encoder.

Requirements:
1. @Configuration @EnableWebSecurity @EnableMethodSecurity (for @PreAuthorize).
2. SecurityFilterChain bean — configure HttpSecurity:
   • CSRF disabled (stateless JWT APIs)
   • SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS
   • permitAll: /api/auth/login, /api/register, /actuator/health
   • authenticated: everything else
   • addFilterBefore(jwtAuthFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class)
3. PasswordEncoder bean — BCryptPasswordEncoder.
4. AuthenticationProvider or DaoAuthenticationProvider wired to UserDetailsService.
5. Optional: corsConfigurationSource for frontend dev.

Key concepts: SecurityFilterChain replaces old WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter. Filters run before controllers — JWT filter extracts Bearer token, validates it, sets SecurityContext. 401 = not authenticated, 403 = authenticated but wrong role.
gh-w9-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Misconfigured SecurityConfiguration is the #1 cause of "403 on everything" or "security not working at all." Every Spring Boot backend job expects you to explain this class in interviews.

Push:
git add SecurityConfiguration.java
git commit -m "Add SecurityConfiguration — JWT filter chain and BCrypt"
git push

Debug tip: Enable logging — logging.level.org.springframework.security=DEBUG — to see which filter rejected a request.

gh-w9-f6.answer:
RoleManagement/ — RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) module for assigning and managing USER, ADMIN, and custom roles.

Project structure:
• RoleManagement/
  - pom.xml or module inside monorepo
  - model/Role.java — enum or entity: USER, ADMIN, MANAGER, DOCTOR, PATIENT
  - model/UserRole.java — optional join table for many-to-many
  - service/RoleService.java — assignRole, removeRole, getUserRoles
  - controller/RoleController.java — admin-only endpoints
  - dto/RoleAssignmentRequest.java — userId, roleName

Endpoints (all require ADMIN role):
• GET /api/admin/roles — list all available roles
• GET /api/admin/users/{userId}/roles — get roles for user
• POST /api/admin/users/{userId}/roles — assign role
• DELETE /api/admin/users/{userId}/roles/{roleName} — remove role
• GET /api/admin/users — list all users (without passwords)

Use @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") on controller class or methods.

Key concepts: RBAC = permissions tied to roles, not individual users. hasRole('ADMIN') checks ROLE_ADMIN authority. Principle of least privilege — users get minimum roles needed.
gh-w9-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Admin panels, internal tools, and multi-tenant SaaS all need role management. @PreAuthorize is cleaner than if-statements in every controller method.

Run (integrated with your security project):
mvn spring-boot:run

Test as admin:
POST /api/admin/users/2/roles
{"roleName":"MANAGER"}
Authorization: Bearer <admin-token>

Test as regular user — should get 403 Forbidden.

Push:
git add RoleManagement/
git commit -m "Add RoleManagement — RBAC with @PreAuthorize"
git push

gh-w9-f7.answer:
ProfileAPI/ — authenticated user profile management endpoints.

Project structure:
• ProfileAPI/ (or package inside main security app)
  - controller/ProfileController.java
  - dto/ProfileResponse.java — id, username, email, roles, createdAt
  - dto/UpdateProfileRequest.java — displayName, phone (no password here)
  - dto/ChangePasswordRequest.java — oldPassword, newPassword, confirmPassword
  - service/ProfileService.java

Endpoints (all require valid JWT):
• GET /api/profile — get current user's profile
• PUT /api/profile — update display name, phone, etc.
• PUT /api/profile/password — change password (uses UserService.changePassword)
• DELETE /api/profile — optional: deactivate own account

Extract current user from SecurityContext:
Authentication auth = SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication();
String username = auth.getName();

Key concepts: Profile endpoints are authenticated but not admin-only — any logged-in user manages their own data. Separate change-password endpoint with old password verification prevents account takeover.
gh-w9-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: "My Account" pages in every app hit profile APIs. Returning a ProfileResponse DTO (not the User entity) prevents accidental password hash leaks.

Run and test:
1. Login → get token
2. GET /api/profile with Bearer token
3. PUT /api/profile with updated fields

Push:
git add ProfileAPI/
git commit -m "Add ProfileAPI — authenticated profile and password change"
git push

gh-w9-f8.answer:
EmployeePortal/ — full employee portal with JWT login, role-based dashboards, and separate admin vs employee endpoints.

Project structure:
• EmployeePortal/
  - pom.xml — web, security, data-jpa, mysql, jjwt
  - model/Employee.java, User.java (linked to employee record)
  - model/Role — EMPLOYEE, ADMIN
  - controller/AuthController.java — login
  - controller/EmployeeDashboardController.java — @PreAuthorize("hasRole('EMPLOYEE')")
  - controller/AdminDashboardController.java — @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
  - service/EmployeeService, DashboardService
  - security/ — JWTConfiguration, SecurityConfiguration, JwtAuthenticationFilter
• application.properties — server.port=8098, database employee_portal_db

Endpoints:
• POST /api/auth/login — public
• GET /api/employee/dashboard — employee sees own tasks, leave balance
• GET /api/employee/profile — own employee record
• GET /api/admin/dashboard — admin sees all employees count, pending approvals
• GET /api/admin/employees — admin lists all employees
• POST /api/admin/employees — admin creates employee + user account
• PUT /api/admin/employees/{id} — admin updates any employee

Key concepts: Same app, different access by role. Employee can only see self; admin sees all. @PreAuthorize at method level is clearer than manual role checks.
gh-w9-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Internal HR portals, support dashboards, and B2B admin panels all use this exact pattern — one API, RBAC gates what each role sees.

Run:
cd EmployeePortal
mvn spring-boot:run

Test flow:
1. Login as employee → GET /api/employee/dashboard → 200
2. Same token → GET /api/admin/employees → 403
3. Login as admin → GET /api/admin/employees → 200

Push:
git add EmployeePortal/
git commit -m "Add EmployeePortal — JWT login with admin/employee RBAC"
git push

gh-w9-f9.answer:
README.md — front page of springboot-week-2-security repository.

Include:
1. Title: # springboot-week-2-security
2. Description — Week 9 Spring Security, JWT, and RBAC projects.
3. Prerequisites — JDK 17+, Maven, MySQL, Postman.
4. Security concepts covered — Authentication vs Authorization, JWT, BCrypt, RBAC, Filter Chain.
5. Projects/files table — AuthenticationAPI, RegistrationAPI, RoleManagement, ProfileAPI, EmployeePortal, plus shared security classes.
6. Environment variables — JWT_SECRET setup.
7. How to run — mvn spring-boot:run per project with ports listed.
8. Testing flow — register → login → use token → test 403 on admin routes.
9. Architecture diagram — Client → JWT Filter → Controller → Service → DB.
10. What you learned — your own words.

Never put real JWT secrets or passwords in README.
gh-w9-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: Security repos get extra scrutiny in interviews. Documenting your auth flow (register → login → Bearer token → RBAC) shows you understand the system, not just copied config.

Push:
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add README — Week 9 Spring Security portfolio"
git push
gh-w9-f9.code:
# springboot-week-2-security

Week 9 — Spring Security, JWT Authentication & Authorization for Prathyu Academy.

## Concepts
- Authentication (who are you?) vs Authorization (what can you do?)
- JWT stateless tokens
- BCrypt password hashing
- RBAC with @PreAuthorize

## Setup
```bash
export JWT_SECRET=your-base64-encoded-256-bit-key
```

## Auth Flow
1. POST /api/register — create account
2. POST /api/auth/login — receive JWT
3. Add header: `Authorization: Bearer <token>`
4. Access protected endpoints

## Projects
| Component | Port | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| AuthenticationAPI | 8091 | Login & JWT validation |
| RegistrationAPI | 8092 | User signup with BCrypt |
| EmployeePortal | 8098 | Admin vs Employee dashboards |

## What I Learned
I implemented JWT-based authentication, hashed passwords with BCrypt, and protected endpoints using Spring Security filter chains and role-based access control.

gh-w9-f10.answer:
Postman Collection.json — Postman collection for all Week 9 security APIs with token handling.

Requirements:
1. Collection name: "Spring Boot Week 9 — Security APIs".
2. Folders: Registration, Authentication, Profile, Role Management (Admin), Employee Portal.
3. Collection variables: baseUrl, authToken (empty initially).
4. Login request — Tests tab script to save token:
   pm.collectionVariables.set("authToken", pm.response.json().token);
5. Protected requests — Authorization header: Bearer {{authToken}}.
6. Include test cases:
   • Register new user → 201
   • Login → 200 + token saved
   • GET /api/profile with token → 200
   • GET /api/admin/users without admin token → 403
   • GET /api/admin/users with admin token → 200
7. Export as Collection v2.1 JSON.

Key concepts: Automating token capture in Postman avoids manual copy-paste during testing. Collection Runner can run the full auth flow sequentially.
gh-w9-f10.realWorld:
Why it matters: QA teams run Postman collections in CI (Newman) to verify auth still works after every deploy. Saving JWT to a variable is standard Postman practice for API test suites.

Import into Postman, run Registration → Login folder first, then protected folders.

Push:
git add "Postman Collection.json"
git commit -m "Add Postman collection with JWT auto-capture"
git push
